caveat: All figures are contested, lag events on the ground, and mix belligerent claims with independent estimates. Treat every number as an order of magnitude, not a fact.
background: The 2026 Iran war began on 28 February when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury — coordinated strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening hours. Iran retaliated against Israel, US bases, and Gulf states; Hezbollah opened a Lebanon front days later. A memorandum signed 17 June at Versailles and Tehran collapsed on 8 July, and fighting has since re-centered on control of the Strait of Hormuz, which the IRGC declared closed on 12 July.

status.headline: Trump weighs a 'massive attack' as the UN warns the war is 'out of control'; House votes to end the war it just funded; oil tops $100
status.detail: The war lurched toward its most dangerous rung and its sharpest political rupture at once. President Trump said he is close to deciding whether to launch 'a massive attack' on Iran — larger than the twelve-night campaign so far and widely read as the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz — as UN Secretary-General António Guterres told the Security Council the Middle East is being pushed to 'the edge of the unimaginable' and 'getting out of control.' In Washington, the House of Representatives passed a war-powers resolution directing Trump to end the conflict — its second such rebuke and the first congressional vote to limit the war since the interim ceasefire collapsed — barely a day after the same chamber adopted a roughly $95bn Republican budget to fund the war and a $1.15tn military bill expanding cooperation with Israel; the House and Senate split on rival war-powers measures, so no binding brake reached Trump's desk, and Senator Rand Paul said the US is 'worse off than we were before.' The funding votes were the House's answer to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's request for nearly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock munitions) atop a war he put at $37.5bn, made to a Senate where Michigan Democrat Gary Peters had told him 'you, sir, are the failure.' Brent crude broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months and shares slid as Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis attacked a Saudi oil tanker in the Red Sea, claimed a second, and threatened to make Bab al-Mandeb — the 'Gate of Tears' — a second live chokepoint; war-risk insurance for Hormuz transits ran roughly four times its five-year average. CENTCOM said it completed a twelfth consecutive night of strikes, which Al Jazeera reported killed two people, with explosions in Sirik, Ahvaz and Bushehr; the US separately signed a civil-nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia. The confirmed US toll stands at 17–18 dead and nearly 100 wounded, with the Guardian naming four of the fallen among '18 US troops killed.'

stats.label: Killed in Iran (Iranian figures)
stats.note: US/Israeli estimates put Iranian military deaths alone above 6,000

stats.label: US service members killed
stats.note: An ABC News/Time tally put the toll at 17; on 23 July the Guardian named four of the fallen among '18 US troops killed' — the count creeping up in a war fought across several countries. Includes those killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase — where the WSJ reports the Iranian missile struck US personnel housing — and Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, NC, killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone

stats.label: US service members wounded
stats.note: The Pentagon admitted nearly 100 US troops have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes, 21 July — a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld

stats.label: Left without water in southern Iran
stats.note: Iranian figure after US strikes on water infrastructure, 18 July

stats.label: Iranian naval vessels destroyed or damaged
Direct US war cost so far
stats.note: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's figure to the Senate on 21 July; he requested roughly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock depleted munitions). On 23 July the House passed a ~$95bn budget to fund the war and a $1.15tn military bill, largely along party lines, both facing an uncertain Senate. A separate running estimate put the broader cost to US taxpayers above $113bn as of June 2026

stats.label: Global oil supply disruption
stats.note: Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of global oil flows

stats.label: Crude oil price
stats.note: Brent broke above $100 a barrel on 23 July — its highest of the war and the first time in two months — as shares slid, after Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers, the Bab al-Mandeb 'Gate of Tears' threat, and Trump's talk of a 'massive attack' on Iran; per the Guardian and CNBC

stats.label: Hormuz war-risk insurance
stats.note: War-risk insurance for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz is running roughly four times its five-year average, per Al Jazeera (23 July) — a cost that flows into freight and, in time, pump prices

stats.label: US gasoline price
stats.note: US average gasoline topped $4 a gallon on 20 July (per ABC News); diesel has passed $5 — the war's cost reaching American drivers

stats.label: Strait of Hormuz status
stats.note: Declared closed by the IRGC on 12 July; now a live shooting zone — US striking to force it open, Iran attacking tankers to keep it shut. On 22 July Trump vowed to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each Iranian attack in the strait; Iran warned it would answer 'eye for an eye' and by blacking out US-host Gulf states

stats.label: Houthi Red Sea blockade
stats.note: Declared against Saudi Arabia 20 July and extended to Bab el-Mandeb by 22 July; on 23 July the Houthis attacked a Saudi oil tanker in the Red Sea and claimed a second — turning the blockade kinetic — and threatened to make Bab el-Mandeb, the 'Gate of Tears,' a second live chokepoint. Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group — a second maritime front alongside Hormuz

stats.label: IAEA safeguards access
stats.note: Iran declared normal safeguards 'legally untenable' after strikes on Natanz; on 18 July it also suspended its commitments to the interim deal with the US

stats.label: 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang)
stats.note: On 22 July Trump threatened to hit this underground complex near Natanz 'very heavily'; it is estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, and little is publicly known about it

stats.label: Bushehr nuclear power plant
stats.note: Satellite images show fresh damage from strikes dated 7–12 July; Bushehr is Iran's largest nuclear plant

actors.members: United States, Israel; Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain after Iranian strikes; support from UK, Jordan, Qatar, Turkey and others
Iran (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, President Pezeshkian), Hezbollah, Houthis, Popular Mobilization Forces, Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad
